Documents evolve continuously.
📑 Policies are updated
⚙️ Procedures change

Updates from Press Gazette's Media Strategy Network USA 📍
Last week, Stibo DX joined executives and media leaders at One World Trade Center, New York, to discuss the strategic, operational, and business shifts shaping the future of news media organizations — alongside voices from CNN, Condé Nast, Hearst, Business Insider, SEMAFOR, and more.
 
Key themes emerging during the day included:
👉 Attention is the new distribution. Media brands are increasingly hosting live events not just for revenue — but as a direct feedback loop with their audiences. First-party engagement is becoming a strategic asset in itself.

👉 AI amplifies journalists — it doesn't replace them. The consensus was clear: the journalist remains the creative driver. AI earns its place in the workflow through research acceleration and reformatting content for the right channel and audience — not through generating the story itself.

👉 New revenue frontiers lie in B2B content and machine audiences. From licensing editorial intelligence to large enterprises, to rethinking how media organisations monetize content consumed by AI agents rather than human readers — the next revenue layer is only beginning to take shape.
 
We're proud to contribute to the conversation meaningfully as sponsors of both the panel discussion and a roundtable session.
 
➡️ Want to explore how the Cue media enterprise platform can support your newsroom's next transformation step? 

Get in touch with us here: https://lnkd.in/eT5Tba_Y

Traditional broadcasting wasn’t built for today’s audience expectations.

When live streaming demand surged, Graham Media Group knew its existing workflows couldn’t keep up. So they reimagined what’s possible with a modern, flexible video infrastructure.

By partnering with Arc XP, GMG transformed its approach to live streaming—moving to a unified, cloud-based video backend and launching a first-of-its-kind mobile broadcasting app that puts the power of live video directly in journalists’ hands.

The impact was immediate:
 🔹 +118% live streams played
 🔹 +130% sessions watched
 🔹 +25% YoY site traffic (within weeks)
 🔹 +185% daily users YoY

From breaking news to major live events, GMG is now equipped to meet audiences in the moment, wherever they are.

Read more about how they made the shift: https://hubs.la/Q0488nNJ0

Arc XP is partnering with TollBit to give publishers something they’ve been missing in the AI era: control.

As AI bots increasingly access publisher content, many organizations are left with limited visibility and even fewer ways to capture its value.

This new integration changes that.

With Arc XP + TollBit, publishers can:
➡️ Monitor and classify AI bot traffic in real time
➡️ Set access rules or block entirely
➡️ Create a clear path to monetize AI-driven content access

Publishers gain the ability to manage how automated agents interact with their content and take a more active role in emerging AI licensing models.

When attendees of the INMA Media Subscriptions Summit toured The Globe and Mail, they got an inside look at a newsroom intentionally structured around one core idea: bringing together editorial, product, marketing, and data to drive reader revenue.

That approach has helped position the Canadian national newspaper as a standout in the subscription space, with close to two-thirds of its revenue coming from readers, primarily through digital subscriptions.

It’s a powerful example of what’s possible when the entire organization is aligned around the audience.

From an audience-first newsroom to deeply embedded data practices and AI-supported workflows, The Globe and Mail continues to set the pace for sustainable subscription growth.

We’re proud to support our customers at The Globe and Mail as they continue to innovate and lead in building a modern, reader-focused news business.

Machine traffic now accounts for more activity on the web than humans. For publishers, that shift raises a big question: who’s benefiting from all that access?

AI systems are constantly crawling publisher sites to train models and power AI-driven search experiences. Yet many organizations still lack visibility into how their content is being accessed, what it’s worth, or how to turn that activity into revenue.

Simply blocking AI bots may protect your content, but it can also reduce visibility in the next generation of search. Leaving the door open, on the other hand, risks giving away valuable journalism with little control, protection, or compensation.

There’s a smarter path forward.

Join us next week for “Beyond Blocking: Building a Smarter AI Monetization Strategy.”

Moderated by Joe Croney, CTO of Arc XP, this executive-level discussion will explore how publishers can move beyond reactive measures and start treating AI access as a strategic opportunity.

🔹 Josh Stone, VP, Business Development & Partnerships, TollBit
🔹 Anabelle Nicoud, Futures Team, Microsoft AI

AI access isn’t just a technical challenge. It’s quickly becoming a defining business model decision for publishers.

Register to join the conversation: https://lnkd.in/g-viJfx8

Are we entering round two of apps?

At Arc XP Connect NYC, Jonny Kaldor, CEO of Pugpig, joined Ariscielle Novicio (New York Post), Kathy Colafemina (Boston Globe Media), and James Cooney (Condé Nast) to explore why mobile apps are stepping back into the spotlight.

The takeaway: while apps may represent a smaller share of total reach, they consistently deliver the most engaged, loyal, and often most valuable audiences.

As publishers shift focus from chasing page views to building deeper relationships, apps are emerging as powerful tools for:
🔹 Strengthening retention and daily habit
🔹 Driving subscriber loyalty from day one
🔹 Creating immersive, high-value ad environments
🔹 Delivering exclusive experiences that spark downloads

It’s not a return to the app strategies of the early 2010s. It’s a more intentional, data-driven approach to building direct audience relationships.

Read the full article: https://lnkd.in/grYYGM-c

This week, Arc XP joined publishers and partners in Hannover for The Future of German Media hosted by MADSACK Mediengruppe! 

A central question carried through every session: How will journalism be financed five years from now? As MADSACK CEO Thomas Düffert noted, the next 3–5 years will determine the next decades of our industry.

There was a shared recognition that while print remains an important and valuable revenue stream today, it can’t carry the weight of journalism’s future alone. Long-term sustainability will depend on scalable digital business models built for growth and efficiency, without losing sight of what matters most: trust.

During the Digital Publishing track, Ioana Blaut took the stage to explore how storytelling is evolving beyond the traditional article. In her session, she shared why stories are no longer the final product, but the foundation for dynamic, interactive experiences that deepen engagement and create new opportunities for growth.

Thank you to MADSACK and the many publishers and partners who made the discussions so impactful. The future of journalism is being shaped right now, and we’re proud to be part of the conversation.

Publishers are being told to block AI bots to protect their content. But block everything, and you risk losing visibility in AI-driven search. Allow full access, and you’re giving away valuable journalism, with no guarantee of traffic or revenue to show for it.

What if you don’t have to choose?

Join us on March 26 at 12PM ET for “Beyond Blocking: Building a Smarter AI Monetization Strategy.”

This executive-level webinar discussion will explore how publishers can move from reactive defense to strategic control by monitoring AI bot traffic, understanding revenue risk, and turning AI access into a measurable opportunity.

Moderated by Joe Croney, CTO of Arc XP, this conversation brings together leaders shaping the future of AI and publishing:

🔹 Josh Stone, VP, Business Development & Partnerships, TollBit
🔹 Anabelle Nicoud, Member of the Futures Team, Microsoft AI

Hear firsthand how they’re thinking about AI access, monetization, and what smarter control looks like for publishers.

AI access isn’t just a technical issue. It’s a business model decision.

Register now to join the conversation: https://lnkd.in/gkrEUQp8

What is the most common mistake athletic departments are going to make as private capital enters college sports?
 
AJ Maestas and Charles Rolston share key questions that administrators must answer:
 
1) How are you spending the money?
2) Are you maintaining optionality for the future?
3) Are incentives properly aligned?
 
Schools must optimize their revenue, and not use the funds to cover up short-term gaps.

Unlike most Olympic programs around the world, Team USA receives zero government funding.
 
That means every medal, every athlete, and every Games cycle is powered by sponsorship, media rights, and philanthropic contributions.
 
Katie Bynum Aznavorian - Chief Strategy and Growth Officer at the USOPC - is tasked with serving the Olympic mission while maintaining real commercial discipline.
 
Beyond just the medal count, Katie details the metrics that show a successful Milan Cortina Games and the work that is already being done for LA28.
 
She also gives her opinion on whether changes to the NCAA and the college sports model will threaten the pipeline for Olympic talent in the U.S.
 
Listen now 🎧: https://lnkd.in/grd-8A6W
 
If you’d like to be featured on the show or want to learn more about the insights discussed, email us at INFO@NVGT.COM.
